title: Product Discovery Maturity

introduction.subtitle: Team maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Product Discovery Maturity helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Continuous Customer Contact, Problem Framing, Experiment Quality, Decision Integration. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How frequently does the product trio or equivalent team engage directly with target users?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Continuous Customer Contact: How frequently does the product trio or equivalent team engage directly with target users
conceptHelp.summary: Continuous Customer Contact means regular direct contact with relevant users or buyers by the people making product decisions. This question narrows the concept to "How frequently does the product trio or equivalent team engage directly with target users?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Continuous Customer Contact matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "How frequently does the product trio or equivalent team engage directly with target users?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

questions.text: How representative is the customer evidence used in product decisions?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How are opportunities framed before solutions are discussed?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Problem Framing; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Problem Framing, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Problem Framing has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Problem Framing is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Problem Framing: How are opportunities framed before solutions are discussed
conceptHelp.summary: Problem Framing means defining the customer problem, context, constraints, and desired outcome before jumping to a solution. This question narrows the concept to "How are opportunities framed before solutions are discussed?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

conceptHelp.title: Experiment Quality: How are discovery tests selected
conceptHelp.summary: Experiment Quality means choosing discovery tests that reduce the riskiest assumptions with credible evidence and clear interpretation rules. This question narrows the concept to "How are discovery tests selected?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Experiment Quality matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.

questions.text: How often does discovery evidence change the roadmap or stop work?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Decision Integration: How often does discovery evidence change the roadmap or stop work
conceptHelp.summary: Decision Integration means whether discovery evidence actually changes roadmap, scope, priority, or strategy rather than living in notes. This question narrows the concept to "How often does discovery evidence change the roadmap or stop work?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Decision Integration matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.

conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "How often does discovery evidence change the roadmap or stop work?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

questions.text: How traceable is the path from insight to product decision?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How reliably does the team speak to non-customers or churned users?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows contact with non-customers and churned users; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of contact with non-customers and churned users, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show contact with non-customers and churned users in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Contact with non-customers and churned users is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Continuous Customer Contact: How reliably does the team speak to non-customers or churned users
conceptHelp.summary: Continuous Customer Contact is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How reliably does the team speak to non-customers or churned users?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Continuous Customer Contact matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
